posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked:
image2820×2820 2.52 MB

I’ve heard/seen William Kelley (and many others) praise this producer so have been moderately interested in trying something from them. About time! Great style and substance here, I would happily drink this any day of the week again. Fantastic value right now, but I reckon that is about to change. If you like white Burgundy and see this, buy all you can (likely not a lot).

  • 2021 Bruno Lorenzon Bourgogne Blanc Clos Alfred - France, Burgundy, Bourgogne Blanc (1.8.2023)
    Quite a distinct lactic malo note on the nose. Not disturbing/negative, but very prominent. Otherwise brightly lemony with some honeydew, yeasty leesiness and quite attractive oakiness. Somehow makes me think of Champagne with its autolytic notes and fruit profile. On the palate it has a great airy mouthfeel, generous savory fruit and just splendid acidity. The oak use really impresses me; this is indeed beautifully textured and the amount of oak and toast level feel like a perfect match for the crisp citrus fruit of the wine. There is a strong chalky feel to it that gives it a sort of Chablis vibe. The finish is long, salty and mouth-watering, making this a mighty tasty and drinkable tipple. Comparing to my previous experience with this part of Burgundy the style here is very different to that of Dureuil-Janthial (not sure how relevant the comparison is). This is lighter, tighter and more electric and closer to my preference by a pretty significant margin. To make another Champagne reference, there is something Bereche-esque about this and I’m loving it. I will definitely be looking to buy more of this bottling and other wines from Lorenzon.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen
posts.cooked: I have also been very impressed with the wines I have tried from him, and have started stocking up before it becomes too late

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked:
Benjamin_Leth_A:

I have also been very impressed with the wines I have tried from him, and have started stocking up before it becomes too late

Yeah, this is one of those times where is it absolutely clear that the prices will be seeing steep increases. I’m seeing three cuvées available at attractive prices and I’m afraid I’m going to have to pull the trigger.

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen
posts.cooked:
I managed to get 3 each of the top cuvees (Piece 13 and Piece 15) in both 19 and 20 - at a very decent price. Do not expect that to continue in the coming vintages, but one can only hope =)

Have not tasted those yet, as I am following the advice of WK and laying down for some years - but all the ‚lesser‘ cuvees I have had so far have been brilliant - especially the white

posts.name: Sh@n Agra

How long are you cellaring for?

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen

So far yes - but will it continue to be?

Not sure yet. Thinking of trying the first one after 5 years and keeping some for 10+

Whats your experience with them?

posts.name: Benjamin Leth Andersen
posts.cooked:
Chapitre is delicious - one of the more easy drinking in the range I believe. Champs Martin more serious and needs a bit of time, but will be amazing.

Give Mont Laurent a 1hr decant and it sings! Sure it will also improve a lot with time however

In summary; buy, buy, buy =)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Le Mont Laurent is from a terrific site in Montagny and well worth following.

Between Lorenzon and the newcomer Maxime Cottenceau, Montagny is finally getting interesting!

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: Amazing wine but the moment to buy it at good value is over or just about over. Need to fine the next one now.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Montagny is finally getting interesting

JM Pillot Les Gouresses and Clos Salomon Le Clou Blanc are also worth mentioning. I’ve found that the Montagny that have impressed me have been able to dial the oak just right to still get the oak spices but retain the necessary freshness.
